topics.title: Sensory Evaluation of food products by 9 point hedonic Scale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL508 - PCFTL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sensory Evaluation of food products by 9 point hedonic Scale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Sensory Evaluation

**Sensory Evaluation** is a scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to food products as perceived through the five senses — **sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing**. As per the KTU 2024 Food Technology syllabus, it is a critical analytical tool employed in the **Quality Assurance (QA)** and **New Product Development (NPD)** divisions of the food industry to quantify consumer acceptance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Standard):**
> Sensory evaluation is defined as "a scientific method used to measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to the sensory properties of food and materials as perceived by the senses of sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing."

### 1.2 Formal Definition: The 9-Point Hedonic Scale

The **9-Point Hedonic Scale**, originally developed by **David Peryam and Frances Pilgrim in 1957** at the Quartermaster Food and Container Institute (USA), is the most widely used **affective (preference/acceptance) sensory test** in the food industry. It measures the **degree of liking** or **disliking** of a food product on a structured bipolar scale ranging from **1 (Dislike extremely)** to **9 (Like extremely)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The scale is **bipolar**, meaning it has a neutral midpoint (score 5) that allows the panelist to express both positive and negative preferences, and is **balanced**, with an equal number of positive and negative categories.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking into a new restaurant and being asked, *"On a scale of 1 to 9, how much did you like the pasta you just ate?"* 

- If you absolutely loved it, you would rate it a **9 (Like extremely)**.
- If it tasted terrible, you would rate it a **1 (Dislike extremely)**.
- If you felt neutral — not bad, not great — you would rate it a **5 (Neither like nor dislike)**.

The 9-Point Hedonic Scale works exactly this way, but with **scientific precision**: it standardizes these intuitive feelings into measurable numbers that food scientists can statistically analyze to predict whether a product will succeed in the market.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called "Hedonic"?** The term **hedonic** is derived from the Greek word *hedonikos* (relating to pleasure), reflecting the scale's focus on measuring **pleasure-based acceptance** rather than objective physical measurements (like pH or moisture content).

### 1.4 Standard Scale Descriptors

| Score | Verbal Category | Hedonic Zone |
|:-----:|:----------------|:-------------|
| **9** | Like extremely | Strong Positive |
| **8** | Like very much | Positive |
| **7** | Like moderately | Positive |
| **6** | Like slightly | Mildly Positive |
| **5** | Neither like nor dislike | Neutral |
| **4** | Dislike slightly | Mildly Negative |
| **3** | Dislike moderately | Negative |
| **2** | Dislike very much | Negative |
| **1** | Dislike extremely | Strong Negative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** A score $\geq$ **6.0** is generally accepted in the food industry as the **threshold for consumer acceptability**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Operational Logic

The 9-Point Hedonic Scale operates on a well-defined sequential logic that transforms subjective human perception into objective, quantifiable data:

1. **Sample Selection and Preparation** — The food product (or multiple variants) is prepared under controlled conditions, coded with **three-digit random numbers** (e.g., 472, 891) to prevent bias.
2. **Panelist Recruitment** — A panel of **untrained, semi-trained, or consumer panelists** (typically 30–100 members) is selected, as this scale measures *natural consumer preference* rather than trained sensory discrimination.
3. **Randomized Presentation** — Samples are presented in a **randomized order** (using a Latin Square or randomization table) to neutralize **order bias** and **carry-over effects**.
4. **Sensory Evaluation** — Each panelist independently scores each sample on the 9-point scale under standardized environmental conditions (e.g., white light, 22°C room temperature).
5. **Data Collection** — Scores are recorded on a structured ballot/scorecard.
6. **Statistical Analysis** — Data is analyzed using **Mean**, **Standard Deviation**, and inferential tests like **ANOVA** or **t-test** to determine significance.
7. **Interpretation and Reporting** — Results are interpreted for product acceptability and reformulation decisions.

### 2.2 Why and How: The Science Behind the Scale

- **Why bipolar?** The bipolar design prevents the **central tendency bias** (where panelists cluster in the middle) by offering balanced positive and negative options.
- **Why 9 points?** Psychophysical research by Peryam & Pilgrim showed that **9 categories** provided optimal **discriminatory power** without overwhelming the panelist's cognitive load.
- **How does it differ from ranking tests?** The hedonic scale measures **intensity of liking**, not just **relative order** of preference.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **key mathematical formulas** required for analyzing 9-Point Hedonic Scale data as per KTU 2024 examination patterns.

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Unit |
|:----------|:--------|:------------|:-----|
| **Mean Hedonic Score** | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$ | Average of all panelist scores | Dimensionless |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_i - \bar{H})^2}{n-1}}$ | Measures score variability | Dimensionless |
| **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_i - \bar{H})^2}{n-1}$ | Square of standard deviation | Dimensionless |
| **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{H}} \times 100$ | Relative variability | Percentage (%) |
| **t-test (Two Samples)** | $t = \dfrac{\vert \bar{H}_1 - \bar{H}_2 \vert}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | Compares two products | Dimensionless |
| **F-statistic (ANOVA)** | $F = \dfrac{MS_{treatment}}{MS_{error}}$ | Compares multiple (≥3) products | Dimensionless |
| **Acceptability Index** | $AI = \dfrac{\bar{H}}{9} \times 100$ | Percentage of maximum possible score | Percentage (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance Threshold:** A result is considered **statistically significant** at $p < 0.05$, which corresponds to a 95% confidence level in KTU lab experiments.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** New product launches (juices, snacks, ready-to-eat meals) are validated using this scale.
- **Quality Control (QC):** Batch-to-batch consistency is monitored in production lines.
- **Shelf-Life Studies:** Acceptability is tracked over storage time to determine expiry.
- **Reformulation:** When a recipe change is made (e.g., sugar reduction), the scale measures if consumer acceptance drops below the threshold.
- **Marketing & Packaging:** The scale data informs whether a product with a new flavor profile can survive market launch.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculation of Mean Hedonic Score and Standard Deviation

**Problem Statement (KTU Lab Report Style):**

A newly developed **strawberry-flavored yogurt** was evaluated by **10 semi-trained panelists** using the 9-Point Hedonic Scale. The raw scores obtained are: **7, 8, 6, 9, 7, 8, 7, 6, 8, 7**. Calculate the **Mean Hedonic Score** and **Standard Deviation**, and interpret the consumer acceptability of the product.

---

#### Step 1: Tabulate the Data

| Panelist (i) | Score ($H_i$) | Deviation ($H_i - \bar{H}$) | Squared Deviation $(H_i - \bar{H})^2$ |
|:------------:|:-------------:|:---------------------------:|:-------------------------------------:|
| 1 | 7 | — | — |
| 2 | 8 | — | — |
| 3 | 6 | — | — |
| 4 | 9 | — | — |
| 5 | 7 | — | — |
| 6 | 8 | — | — |
| 7 | 7 | — | — |
| 8 | 6 | — | — |
| 9 | 8 | — | — |
| 10 | 7 | — | — |

---

#### Step 2: Calculate the Sum of Scores

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i = 7 + 8 + 6 + 9 + 7 + 8 + 7 + 6 + 8 + 7 = 73$$

*Explanation:* We sum all 10 individual panelist scores to obtain the total hedonic score sum.

---

#### Step 3: Calculate the Mean Hedonic Score

$$\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n} = \frac{73}{10} = 7.3$$

*Explanation:* Divide the total sum (73) by the number of panelists (n = 10). This is the **central tendency** of the data.

---

#### Step 4: Calculate Each Deviation from the Mean

We now subtract the mean (7.3) from each individual score:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 - \bar{H} &= 7.0 - 7.3 = -0.3 \\
H_2 - \bar{H} &= 8.0 - 7.3 = +0.7 \\
H_3 - \bar{H} &= 6.0 - 7.3 = -1.3 \\
H_4 - \bar{H} &= 9.0 - 7.3 = +1.7 \\
H_5 - \bar{H} &= 7.0 - 7.3 = -0.3 \\
H_6 - \bar{H} &= 8.0 - 7.3 = +0.7 \\
H_7 - \bar{H} &= 7.0 - 7.3 = -0.3 \\
H_8 - \bar{H} &= 6.0 - 7.3 = -1.3 \\
H_9 - \bar{H} &= 8.0 - 7.3 = +0.7 \\
H_{10} - \bar{H} &= 7.0 - 7.3 = -0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The deviation represents how far each panelist's score is from the mean. Positive values mean "above average liking"; negative values mean "below average liking."

---

#### Step 5: Square the Deviations and Sum Them

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_i - \bar{H})^2 &= (-0.3)^2 + (0.7)^2 + (-1.3)^2 + (1.7)^2 + (-0.3)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.7)^2 + (-0.3)^2 + (-1.3)^2 + (0.7)^2 + (-0.3)^2 \\
&= 0.09 + 0.49 + 1.69 + 2.89 + 0.09 \\
&\quad + 0.49 + 0.09 + 1.69 + 0.49 + 0.09 \\
&= 8.10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Squaring removes negative signs and emphasizes larger deviations.

---

#### Step 6: Calculate the Variance and Standard Deviation

The sample variance uses $n-1$ (Bessel's correction):

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_i - \bar{H})^2}{n-1} = \frac{8.10}{10-1} = \frac{8.10}{9} = 0.90$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.90} \approx 0.949$$

*Explanation:* The standard deviation of approximately **0.949** indicates that the panelists' scores are tightly clustered around the mean, showing strong agreement on acceptability.

---

#### Step 7: Interpret the Result

- **Mean Hedonic Score = 7.3**, which falls between "Like moderately" (7) and "Like very much" (8).
- **Standard Deviation = 0.949** (low, indicating high panelist consensus).
- **Verdict:** The strawberry yogurt is **well-accepted** by consumers and is recommended for further development and market launch.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Acceptability Index Calculation

Using the same dataset ($\bar{H} = 7.3$):

$$AI = \frac{\bar{H}}{9} \times 100 = \frac{7.3}{9} \times 100 \approx 81.11\%$$

*Explanation:* An Acceptability Index of **81.11%** indicates that the product achieved over 80% of the maximum possible hedonic score — a strong commercial indicator.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Between Two Products

**Problem:** Compare the acceptability of two fruit juices (A and B) using a t-test. Use the following hypothetical data:

| Statistic | Juice A | Juice B |
|:----------|:-------:|:-------:|
| Sample size (n) | 12 | 12 |
| Mean ($\bar{H}$) | 7.5 | 6.8 |
| Variance ($\sigma^2$) | 0.64 | 0.81 |

**Solution:**

$$
t = \frac{\vert \bar{H}_A - \bar{H}_B \vert}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_A^2}{n_A} + \dfrac{\sigma_B^2}{n_B}}} = \frac{\vert 7.5 - 6.8 \vert}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.64}{12} + \dfrac{0.81}{12}}} = \frac{0.7}{\sqrt{0.1208}} = \frac{0.7}{0.3476} \approx 2.01
$$

*Explanation:* The calculated t-value (2.01) is compared against the critical t-value from the t-distribution table at $df = n_1 + n_2 - 2 = 22$ and $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed), which is **2.074**. Since 2.01 < 2.074, the difference is **not statistically significant** at the 5% level — meaning both juices are equally acceptable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Sensory Evaluation Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Sample Preparation] --> B[Step 2: Three-Digit Random Coding]
    B --> C[Step 3: Panelist Selection]
    C --> D[Step 4: Randomized Sample Presentation]
    D --> E[Step 5: Panelist Scores Sample on 9 Point Scale]
    E --> F[Step 6: Data Collection via Scorecard]
    F --> G[Step 7: Statistical Analysis Mean SD ANOVA]
    G --> H[Step 8: Interpretation and Reporting]
    H --> I{Mean Score greater than or equal to 6}
    I -- Yes --> J[Product Accepted for Market]
    I -- No --> K[Reformulation Required]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the 9-Point Hedonic Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS[POSITIVE LIKING ZONE]
        A1[9 Like extremely]
        A2[8 Like very much]
        A3[7 Like moderately]
        A4[6 Like slightly]
    end
    subgraph NEU[NEUTRAL ZONE]
        A5[5 Neither like nor dislike]
    end
    subgraph NEG[NEGATIVE LIKING ZONE]
        A6[4 Dislike slightly]
        A7[3 Dislike moderately]
        A8[2 Dislike very much]
        A9[1 Dislike extremely]
    end
    A9 --> A8 --> A7 --> A6 --> A5 --> A4 --> A3 --> A2 --> A1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Sensory Evaluation Test Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensory Evaluation Tests] --> B[Affective Tests Consumer Acceptance]
    A --> C[Discriminative Tests Trained Panel]
    A --> D[Descriptive Tests Expert Panel]
    B --> B1[Hedonic 9 Point Scale]
    B --> B2[Paired Preference Test]
    B --> B3[Acceptance Test]
    C --> C1[Triangle Test]
    C --> C2[Paired Comparison Test]
    C --> C3[Duo Trio Test]
    D --> D1[Flavor Profile Analysis]
    D --> D2[Quantitative Descriptive Analysis]
    D --> D3[Texture Profile Method]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the 9-Point Hedonic Scale. List all nine categories with their corresponding numerical scores.**

**Model Answer:**

The 9-Point Hedonic Scale is a structured, bipolar affective sensory measurement tool developed by **Peryam and Pilgrim (1957)** to quantify the degree of liking or disliking of a food product.

The nine categories are:

| Score | Category |
|:-----:|:---------|
| 9 | Like extremely |
| 8 | Like very much |
| 7 | Like moderately |
| 6 | Like slightly |
| 5 | Neither like nor dislike |
| 4 | Dislike slightly |
| 3 | Dislike moderately |
| 2 | Dislike very much |
| 1 | Dislike extremely |

---

#### **Question 2 [3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a hedonic test and a discriminative test in sensory evaluation. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hedonic Test | Discriminative Test |
|:----------|:-------------|:--------------------|
| **Purpose** | Measures degree of liking/acceptance | Detects sensory differences between samples |
| **Panel Type** | Untrained consumer panel | Trained expert panel |
| **Panelist Count** | 30–100 (large) | 10–25 (small) |
| **Example** | 9-Point Hedonic Scale | Triangle Test, Paired Comparison Test |
| **Output** | Mean acceptability score | Number of correct responses |

---

### 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define sensory evaluation. Explain any **four types of sensory tests** with suitable examples from the food industry.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Sensory evaluation is a scientific method used to **measure, analyze, and interpret human responses** to the sensory characteristics of food (appearance, aroma, taste, texture, and sound) as perceived by the five human senses.

**Four Types of Sensory Tests:**

1. **Affective (Preference/Acceptance) Tests:** Measure *how much* a consumer likes a product.
   - *Example:* 9-Point Hedonic Scale, Paired Preference Test.
   - *Application:* Used in marketing research to predict commercial success.

2. **Discriminative Tests:** Detect whether a *sensory difference* exists between two or more samples.
   - *Example:* Triangle Test (panelist identifies the odd sample from three).
   - *Application:* Quality control — verifying if a reformulation changed the sensory profile.

3. **Descriptive Tests:** Identify and quantify *specific sensory attributes* (sweet, sour, crunchy, etc.).
   - *Example:* Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA), Flavor Profile Method.
   - *Application:* R&D — pinpointing the cause of off-flavors.

4. **Threshold Tests:** Determine the *minimum concentration* at which a stimulus can be detected.
   - *Example:* Detection threshold and recognition threshold for bitterness in caffeine.
   - *Application:* Flavor ingredient formulation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **procedure** for conducting sensory evaluation using the 9-Point Hedonic Scale. List **four precautions** to be observed during the test.

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure:**

1. **Sample Preparation:** Prepare samples under identical conditions (temperature, portion size, container).
2. **Random Coding:** Assign each sample a unique three-digit random number to eliminate brand bias.
3. **Panel Selection:** Recruit 30–100 untrained or semi-trained consumer panelists.
4. **Environmental Control:** Conduct the test in a quiet, well-lit (white light), odor-free booth at 22 ± 2°C.
5. **Randomized Presentation:** Serve samples in a randomized order (Latin Square design).
6. **Scoring:** Each panelist independently rates each sample on the 9-point scale.
7. **Water Rinse:** Provide water and unsalted crackers between samples to cleanse the palate.
8. **Data Recording:** Collect scorecards and tabulate data.
9. **Statistical Analysis:** Compute mean, standard deviation, and run ANOVA/t-test.
10. **Interpretation:** Report acceptability based on mean score and statistical significance.

**Precautions:**

1. **No Smoking or Eating** for at least 30 minutes before the test.
2. **Avoid Strong Odors** (perfume, coffee) in the testing area.
3. **Prevent Panelist Communication** to avoid bias.
4. **Standardize Sample Temperature** to prevent variations in perceived taste/texture.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A newly developed **chocolate-flavored milk** was evaluated by **15 panelists** using the 9-Point Hedonic Scale. The scores obtained are:
**8, 7, 9, 6, 8, 7, 9, 8, 7, 8, 9, 7, 8, 9, 7.** Calculate the **mean hedonic score**, **standard deviation**, **coefficient of variation**, and **acceptability index**. Interpret the results.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Sum of Scores**

$$\sum H_i = 8+7+9+6+8+7+9+8+7+8+9+7+8+9+7 = 117$$

**Step 2: Mean Hedonic Score**

$$\bar{H} = \frac{117}{15} = 7.8$$

**Step 3: Sum of Squared Deviations from Mean**

| $H_i$ | $H_i - 7.8$ | $(H_i - 7.8)^2$ |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:----------------:|
| 8 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 7 | -0.8 | 0.64 |
| 9 | 1.2 | 1.44 |
| 6 | -1.8 | 3.24 |
| 8 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 7 | -0.8 | 0.64 |
| 9 | 1.2 | 1.44 |
| 8 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 7 | -0.8 | 0.64 |
| 8 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 9 | 1.2 | 1.44 |
| 7 | -0.8 | 0.64 |
| 8 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 9 | 1.2 | 1.44 |
| 7 | -0.8 | 0.64 |

$$\sum (H_i - 7.8)^2 = 12.40$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{12.40}{15-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{12.40}{14}} = \sqrt{0.8857} \approx 0.941$$

**Step 5: Coefficient of Variation**

$$CV = \frac{0.941}{7.8} \times 100 \approx 12.06\%$$

**Step 6: Acceptability Index**

$$AI = \frac{7.8}{9} \times 100 \approx 86.67\%$$

**Interpretation:**
The mean score of **7.8** falls between "Like very much" and "Like moderately," indicating **excellent consumer acceptability**. The low CV (12.06%) and low SD (0.941) confirm strong panelist consensus. The product is **recommended for commercial launch**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of the 9-Point Hedonic Scale. How does it differ from the **5-Point and 7-Point** hedonic scales?

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**

1. **Simple and easy to administer** — even untrained consumers can use it.
2. **Bipolar and balanced** — prevents central tendency bias.
3. **High discriminatory power** — 9 points allow finer gradation of preference.
4. **Versatile** — applicable to all food categories (beverages, snacks, dairy, etc.).
5. **Standardized** — enables statistical comparison between studies.
6. **Widely accepted** — referenced in international food standards.

**Limitations:**

1. **Doesn't identify reasons** for liking/disliking (e.g., too salty, too sweet).
2. **Cultural bias** — descriptors may be interpreted differently across cultures.
3. **Cannot detect small differences** between very similar products.
4. **Hedonic vocabulary fatigue** — panelists may overuse middle categories.
5. **No information on "just-about-right"** intensity of attributes.

**Comparison with Other Scales:**

| Feature | 5-Point | 7-Point | **9-Point** |
|:--------|:-------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| Discriminatory power | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| Central tendency bias | High | Moderate | **Low** |
| Panelist fatigue | Low | Moderate | **Moderate** |
| Industry adoption | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sensory Evaluation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's Correction:** Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in the variance formula — **loses 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Not interpreting the result:** Computing the mean is not enough; you MUST interpret it in terms of acceptability (≥ 6.0 = accepted).
> 3. **Mixing up "Hedonic" with "Discriminative":** Hedonic measures liking; Discriminative measures difference. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. **Missing precautions:** Always mention 2–4 precautions (water rinse, no communication, sample coding, temperature control) to secure full marks on procedure questions.
> 5. **Incorrect critical value usage:** In t-test problems, always state the degrees of freedom ($df = n_1 + n_2 - 2$) and the significance level ($\alpha = 0.05$).
> 6. **No mention of randomized design:** Examiners expect the term "**Latin Square / Randomized Block Design**" for sample presentation.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hedonic Scale = Affective Test** (measures *liking*, not *difference*).
- ✅ **Developed by Peryam & Pilgrim (1957)** in the USA.
- ✅ **Scale Range:** 1 (Dislike extremely) → 9 (Like extremely), with **5 (Neither like nor dislike)** as the neutral midpoint.
- ✅ **Acceptability Threshold:** Mean score $\geq$ **6.0** = Product accepted; Mean score $<$ **6.0** = Reformulation needed.
- ✅ **Acceptability Index (AI):** $AI = \dfrac{\bar{H}}{9} \times 100$ — represents the percentage of maximum possible liking.
- ✅ **Panel Size:** 30–100 **untrained/semi-trained consumer** panelists (not trained experts).
- ✅ **Sample Coding:** Always use **three-digit random numbers** (e.g., 472, 891) to prevent brand bias.
- ✅ **Standard Deviation Formula:** Use $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) for sample variance.
- ✅ **CV Interpretation:** $CV < 15\%$ = strong panelist agreement; $CV > 30\%$ = high variability, poor consensus.
- ✅ **Key Statistical Tools:** **Mean**, **SD**, **t-test** (2 samples), **ANOVA** (≥ 3 samples).
- ✅ **Procedure Keywords:** *Randomized presentation → Water rinse → Controlled environment → Independent scoring → Statistical analysis*.
- ✅ **Precautions:** No smoking/eating 30 min prior; no perfumes; standardized sample temperature; panelists must not communicate.
- ✅ **Hedonic ≠ Just-About-Right (JAR):** JAR is a separate test measuring ideal attribute intensity.
- ✅ **Three Major Sensory Test Categories:** *Affective (Hedonic), Discriminative (Triangle), Descriptive (QDA)*.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the 9-Point Scale:** **"D-3-2-1-5-L-3-2-1"** → *Dislike (3 negative + 1 neutral + 5 positive) Like* — Always remember **5 categories on the "Like" side** and **3 categories on the "Dislike" side**, plus the **neutral 5**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
